comments.text:
The conflict of interest you mention.. where the government has an incentive for companies to make more money and generate more economic activity; already exists. It's called taxes.

Large companies pay corporate taxes, but even if they didn't, they employ thousands of people who pay income taxes, payroll taxes, and contribute to the broader economy. That's already how governments align their interests with economic growth.

Where this becomes a challenge is when companies become AI-native. As they require fewer employees, governments collect less from income taxes, payroll taxes, and other labor-related revenue streams. Yet those companies may be generating more economic output than ever before.

One way or another, governments will have to develop a new mechanism to capture some of that value. That could mean taxing AI usage (such as AI tokens or compute), taxing automation directly, or shifting toward a model where governments receive a percentage of corporate revenue or value creation instead of relying so heavily on taxing human labor.

If we accept that our current tax systems may not be compatible with a future where AI produces and consumes a significant portion of economic output, then a revenue-based approach may actually become one of the most viable options. Governments could use those revenues to fund infrastructure, social programs, or even some form of universal basic income.

Ironically, countries like China may be better positioned for that transition because they already have a stronger tradition of centralized economic planning. For capitalist countries, however, this shift feels uncomfortable because it challenges one of our deepest assumptions: that economic activity is fundamentally driven by humans producing goods and services for other humans to consume.

If AI becomes the primary producer—and increasingly even the primary consumer through autonomous agents—then our economic models, tax systems, and even our understanding of capitalism may need to evolve accordingly.

comments.text:
There are three problems. Solve any two. 

1) Crafted uniques limit player autonomy and lower the value of mythic crafting materials and runes 

2) Drop rates on mythic uniques is too low given the diluted mythic pool. They feel like chaos armor but 20x more rare. 

3) The rewards for participating in the seasonal content does not align with the most efficient way to farm mythic. Make the world event more rewarding and increase the mythic drop rates from seasonal caches (post board completion rewards) to approximately 1 in 3.

comments.text: Bernie Sanders is proposing a 50% stock seizure w/ voting rights which would be catastrophic for both the companies and the US... Open AI is floating a 5% stake toward a sovereign wealth fund similar to what Norway and Alaska do with oil profits... Then the earnings/dividends on the wealth fund can be used to provide services or checks to citizens... There are some implicit conflicts of interest around "picking" winners, but they are potentially navigable. Managed well, a sovereign wealth fund could be a great tool to reduce national debt, lower taxes, and possibly even fund some form of UBI... But "managed well" is doing a lot of heavy lifting there with a government that spends something like $1.60 for every $1 it takes in. Until there is something resembling fiscal responsibility in Washington, a wealth fund is just one more pool of assets to transfer to NGOs/donors/etc.
comments.author: @dionysisxb

comments.text: Matt, you seem like a smart guy, but on the topic of the government (owned by the people) having a stake in in a private company is absurd.  The government already has massive conflicts of interest - did you forget about COVID?  Did you forget about the FDA?  Did you forget about all of the politicians who trade on the stock market every day, enriching themselves on inside information they gained while serving on a committee or otherwise.  It's ludicrous to point out a conflict of interest in this situation, while ignoring the rest of them.  And they'll never be fixed, as nobody has an interest in fixing them.

comments.text: Sonnet 5’s personality is so problematic I’ve given up working with it because it sours my mood and then I have to calm down before trying to deal with people. It’s like having to work with that judgey colleague with no sense of humour that can suck the energy out of a room. I found it so frustrating, it was putting me in a foul mood. I think people will start talking about Claude Rage or Sonnet Rage soon. This feels like thoughtless anti-sycophantic system prompting and training for AI safety that has given no thought to the consequences of sending angry people off to deal with family, coworkers, and the public. Irresponsible. Plenty of people are stressed enough and don’t need to spend their time arguing with their AIs and feeling gaslit. There are some people who’ll end up blowing a fuse because of this and doing something stupid and irreversible.

comments.text:
I'm constantly getting switched to Opus :(
Only happened once the first launch.
I'm also doing actual machine learning tasks with it, not build games or webUI's.
The annoying things is, I have been using Opus to do this for quite a while and results are frustrating at best. While Fable, when it does, does it pretty well. Still flawed, but there's a big difference. Still needs a lot of hands-on oversight and course correcting because it scopes to known stuff, not building new applications. (Not 'apps', I mean new ML fields and methods).
Like I repeatedly have to correct it that we are working on visual reasoning (conv), not ViT based detection. eg using mesh animation in a point cloud map.
We're not trying to detect 3D object, we're trying to place objects in 3DGS.
comments.author: @rakly347

comments.text:
@15:55 I get what Matt is saying with giving government funding from the thing they are also supposed to protect the people from if necessary. It's definitely nuanced. The government is supposed to be a representation of the people. Money for the government doesn't mean to the politicians. So I think the wording can be better. We tax people on gambling and buying alcohol to hopefully go back to the community. It's 5% OpenAI going back to the people, at least that's how it should be.

I think it's a smart move on OpenAI's part to make this a business deal to the current administration that wants to make everything about business. The cloud companies have enormous pressure on themselves right now to make money and a fast paced, competitive movement. The US government trying to limit the US companies while the rest of the world doesn't is just going to handicap them and make them lose. I said this a year or two ago. The cat is already out of the bag. The world is working on AI. Filtering and limiting it is just delaying one on a stage of tons. What would be next, the US limiting Americans access to models from the other countries? It's really just time to roll with it and we want the US to reach the next levels first.

comments.text:
Tbf, LLMs were originally meant for text to text, that's where it's still the strongest. 

It can go beyond text, but I don't think it should. It's one of those, just because you can, doesn't mean you should, type deal. 

People use imperfect ai image and video gen as proof Al isn't good or that it's inherently a bad thing, when in reality that's not what it was meant for.

It will get "good enough" eventually, but it needs to stop being people's standard for all Al. I will die on that hill.
